related_task_bank: Complete Benchmark Task Bank for Algebra Tutoring Evaluation
purpose: Curated instructional corpus for retrieval-grounded algebra tutoring in the learner-state-aware pedagogical RAG condition.
created_for: Controlled comparison of prompt-only baseline LLM tutor and learner-state-aware pedagogical RAG tutor.
notes: Each corpus chunk is intentionally small and pedagogically meaningful.
Chunks are aligned with benchmark task topics, subskills, misconception tags, and response modes.
This seed corpus is designed for local development and later AWS Bedrock Knowledge Base ingestion.
No private student data is included.

chunks.title: Combining like terms with variables
chunks.content: Like terms have the same variable part raised to the same power. For example, 3x and 5x are like terms because both contain x. To combine them, add the coefficients: 3x + 5x = 8x. A constant term such as -2 does not combine with x-terms because it has no variable.

chunks.title: Why 3x plus 5x is not 6x
chunks.content: A common error is adding 3x and 5x incorrectly. The coefficients 3 and 5 tell how many x-groups there are. Three x-groups plus five x-groups gives eight x-groups, so 3x + 5x = 8x, not 6x.

chunks.title: Distributive property before combining like terms
chunks.subskill: distributive property and combining like terms
chunks.content: When simplifying an expression with parentheses, distribute first, then combine like terms. Example: 4(a + 3) - 2a becomes 4a + 12 - 2a. Then combine the like terms 4a and -2a to get 2a + 12.

chunks.title: Distribution over subtraction
chunks.content: The distributive property applies to every term inside the parentheses, including negative terms. In 3(x - 4), the expression x - 4 means x plus negative 4. So 3(x - 4) becomes 3x - 12 because 3 times -4 is -12.

chunks.title: Negative distribution with grouped expressions
chunks.content: When subtracting a grouped expression, the subtraction affects the entire group. In 6y - 2(3y - 5), the multiplier is -2, not just 2. Distribute -2 to both terms: -2 times 3y is -6y, and -2 times -5 is +10. The expression becomes 6y - 6y + 10, which simplifies to 10.

chunks.title: Like and unlike terms
chunks.content: Terms can be combined only when they are like terms. Variable terms such as 5m and 2m can combine because both have m. Constants such as -3 and 9 can combine because neither has a variable. But 5m and 9 cannot combine because one has a variable and the other does not.

chunks.title: Inverse operations and isolating a variable
chunks.content: To solve an equation, use inverse operations to isolate the variable. Addition is undone by subtraction, and subtraction is undone by addition. In x + 7 = 15, subtract 7 from both sides because the goal is to undo the +7 attached to x.

chunks.title: Balance principle in equations
chunks.content: An equation stays true only when the same operation is performed on both sides. This is called preserving equality. If you subtract 2n from one side, you must subtract 2n from the other side too. The goal is to keep the equation balanced while moving toward isolating the variable.

chunks.title: Solving two-step equations
chunks.content: For a two-step equation such as 2x + 3 = 11, first undo addition or subtraction, then undo multiplication or division. Subtract 3 from both sides to get 2x = 8. Then divide both sides by 2 to get x = 4.

chunks.title: Why not divide too early in a two-step equation
chunks.content: A common mistake in 2x + 3 = 11 is dividing 11 by 2 before handling the +3. The expression on the left is not only 2x; it is 2x + 3. First undo the +3, then divide by 2.

chunks.title: Distribution in equations
chunks.content: In an equation such as 3(x - 2) = 12, you may either divide both sides by 3 first or distribute first. If distributing, multiply 3 by every term inside the parentheses: 3(x - 2) becomes 3x - 6, not 3x - 2.

chunks.title: Variables on both sides
chunks.content: When variables appear on both sides, collect variable terms on one side and constants on the other while preserving equality. Example: 4n + 6 = 2n + 18. Subtract 2n from both sides to get 2n + 6 = 18. Then subtract 6 from both sides to get 2n = 12. Finally divide by 2, so n = 6.

chunks.title: Substitution means replacing a variable with a value
chunks.content: Substitution means replacing a variable with the given value. In algebra, 2x means 2 times x, not the two-digit number formed by writing 2 next to x. If x = 3, then 2x + 5 becomes 2(3) + 5.

chunks.title: Order of operations with exponents after substitution
chunks.content: After substituting a value, follow the order of operations. Exponents are evaluated before subtraction. For a² - 4 when a = 5, substitute first to get 5² - 4. Then square 5 to get 25, and finally subtract 4 to get 21.

chunks.title: Substitution with subtraction
chunks.content: When substituting into an expression with subtraction, keep the operation signs carefully. For 3p - q when p = 4 and q = 7, substitute to get 3(4) - 7. Compute 3(4) = 12, then 12 - 7 = 5.

chunks.title: Substitution with parentheses
chunks.content: Parentheses show that the expression inside should be treated as one group. For 2(x + y), substitute both values first: 2(3 + 6). Add inside the parentheses to get 2(9), then multiply to get 18.

chunks.title: Correcting the move-across misconception
chunks.content: The phrase 'move it across and change the sign' can hide the real reasoning. A better explanation is inverse operations. In 2x + 5 = 17, the +5 is undone by subtracting 5 from both sides. This gives 2x = 12, then x = 6.

chunks.title: Every term inside parentheses must receive the multiplier
chunks.content: When using the distributive property, the multiplier applies to every term inside the parentheses. For 3(x + 4), multiply 3 by x and also multiply 3 by 4. The result is 3x + 12, not 3x + 4.

chunks.title: Combining unlike terms in worked solutions
chunks.content: A variable term and a constant term cannot be combined into one term. For example, 4x + 7 cannot become 11x because 7 is not an x-term. Only terms with the same variable part can be combined.

chunks.title: Checking each step in a worked solution
chunks.content: To identify an error in a worked solution, compare each line with the line before it. Ask: What operation changed? Was it applied to every required term? Was the same operation done to both sides of an equation? Are only like terms being combined?

chunks.title: Interpreting symbolic relationships
chunks.content: A coefficient tells how many groups of a variable quantity are present. In an expression such as 3x, the 3 means three groups of x. It does not mean the variable is replaced by 3 unless the problem explicitly gives x = 3.

chunks.title: Understanding equivalent expressions
chunks.content: Equivalent expressions may look different but have the same value for every allowed value of the variable. For example, 2(x + y) and 2x + 2y are equivalent because distributing 2 gives the same total.

chunks.title: Reading algebra expressions as structure
chunks.content: Algebra expressions have structure. Parentheses show groups, coefficients show multiplication, exponents show repeated multiplication, and plus or minus signs separate terms. Reading the structure first helps prevent procedural errors.

chunks.title: Guiding question for combining like terms
chunks.content: Before combining terms, ask: Do these terms have the same variable part? If yes, combine the coefficients. If no, keep them separate.

chunks.title: Guiding question for solving equations
chunks.content: When solving an equation, ask: What operation is currently being done to the variable, and what inverse operation will undo it while keeping both sides balanced?

chunks.title: Guiding question for distribution
chunks.content: When distributing, ask: What is the multiplier outside the parentheses, and did I multiply it by every term inside the parentheses, including negative terms?
